# Python Enterprise CRUD Demo

This documentation walks through a small enterprise application demo. 

The example focuses on a B2B discount maintenance scenario and demonstrates how Python applications are typically structured when they read from and write to a Microsoft SQL Server database.

::: {.overview-grid}
::: {.overview-item}
::: {.overview-label}
Stack
:::
Python 3.13, `uv`, Streamlit, `pandas`, `sqlalchemy`, `pyodbc`
:::

::: {.overview-item}
::: {.overview-label}
Database
:::
MSSQL Server with role-based views for data access
:::
:::

## Course Files

This book uses three GitHub repositories. Start with the small examples first, then move to the full CRUD project.

| Step | Repository | Purpose |
| --- | --- | --- |
| 1 | [mssql-example](https://github.com/kirenz/mssql-example) | Test a plain Python connection to Microsoft SQL Server |
| 2 | [streamlit-mssql](https://github.com/kirenz/streamlit-mssql) | Run a small Streamlit app that queries SQL Server |
| 3 | [sopra](https://github.com/kirenz/sopra) | Work with the full layered CRUD application |

Download or clone the full project when you reach the main application part. The repository contains all Python files used in this book, the Quarto source files, and `.env.example`.

- [Download ZIP](https://github.com/kirenz/sopra/archive/refs/heads/main.zip)
- [Open GitHub repository](https://github.com/kirenz/sopra)

If you use Git:

```bash
git clone https://github.com/kirenz/sopra.git
cd sopra
uv sync
```

If you download the ZIP file, unzip the folder, open it in Visual Studio Code, and then run:

```bash
uv sync
```

The local `.env` file is not part of the download. Create it from `.env.example` and add the course-specific SQL Server credentials.

:::{.callout-tip}
Keep the three repositories in separate folders. Do not copy files from the warm-up repositories into the full CRUD project unless an exercise explicitly asks you to do so.
:::

## Recommended Learning Path

1. Use `mssql-example` to verify that the ODBC driver, `.env` file, and SQL Server login work.
2. Use `streamlit-mssql` to run a small browser-based SQL query interface.
3. Use this `sopra` project to study the larger CRUD application with presentation, business logic, and data access layers.

## Learning Goals

By the end of this project, students should be able to:

- set up a Python 3.13 project with `uv`
- connect to Microsoft SQL Server with `pyodbc`, `sqlalchemy`, and `pandas`
- implement typical CRUD-style flows in a Streamlit user interface
- handle database errors and validation messages in a user-friendly way

## Business Scenario

The application manages quantity-based B2B discounts. Sales staff need an internal tool to:

- view existing discount rules
- add a new discount for a selected customer
- edit an existing discount if business conditions change

The SQL Server already exposes role-based views for this workflow, so the Python application does not work directly on the base table. This mirrors a common enterprise setup where database permissions are restricted through views.

## Architecture Overview

```{mermaid}
flowchart TD
    U["User in Browser"] --> A["Presentation Layer<br>app.py<br>Streamlit tabs"]
    A --> B["Business Logic Layer<br>services/discount_service.py<br>validation and rules"]
    B --> C["Data Access Layer<br>repositories/db.py<br>repositories/discount_repo.py"]
    C --> D["Role-Based Views<br>list_views / ins_views / upd_views<br>dbo.LOV_CUSTOMER"]
    D --> E["SQL Server Database<br>ERPDEV"]
```

## Database Objects Used in the Demo

| Purpose | Object |
| --- | --- |
| Read discount overview | `list_views.V_LIST_B2B_DISCOUNT` |
| Insert new discount | `ins_views.V_INS_B2B_DISCOUNT` |
| Update existing discount | `upd_views.V_UPD_B2B_DISCOUNT` |
| Fill customer dropdown | `dbo.LOV_CUSTOMER` |

## Project Structure

```text
.
├── app.py
├── models/discount.py
├── repositories/db.py
├── repositories/discount_repo.py
├── services/discount_service.py
├── sql_example.py
└── README.md
```

## What You Should Notice

- `app.py` contains no raw SQL. The presentation layer only deals with user interaction.
- `discount_service.py` contains no Streamlit code. It focuses on validation and business meaning.
- `discount_repo.py` is the only place where SQL statements are written.
- `.env` keeps credentials out of the source code.

:::{.callout-tip}
This separation is not just a style preference. It makes the application easier to test, easier to explain in class, and easier to extend later.
:::

## Suggested Workflow

1. Complete the operating-system-specific setup steps.
2. Configure your `.env` file.
3. Test the MSSQL connection with `sql_example.py`.
4. Start the Streamlit application with `uv run streamlit run app.py`.
5. Inspect how each UI action maps to the business and data layers.
